summary: DHH compiles a comprehensive FAQ addressing the most common questions about 37signals' cloud exit, one year after completing the migration. He systematically dismantles cloud marketing claims — that it reduces headcount, that 'cloud native' saves money, that security is better, and that you need elite engineers to run your own hardware. The company spent $600,000 on Dell servers, already recouped that investment, and projects $7 million in savings over five years. He argues that running your own hardware in 2023 is operationally similar to cloud thanks to containerization, and that the cloud premium is increasingly unjustified as Moore's Law drives down hardware costs without corresponding cloud price reductions.
key_points: The cloud exit required no change in team composition — the claim that cloud reduces operations headcount is a 'central deceit' of cloud marketing
Hardware ownership is operationally similar to cloud: white-glove data center services handle physical setup, containerization handles deployment, and the same redundancy patterns apply
Moore's Law benefits hardware buyers directly but cloud providers rarely pass savings through, making the cloud a worse deal every year
Serverless and 'cloud native' architectures don't save money at scale — buying single-wrapped sugar cubes costs more than buying a pound
The $600,000 hardware investment was already paid off by cloud savings within months, making the five-year $7M projection conservative
money_quotes: This is the central deceit of the cloud marketing, that it's all going to be so much easier that you hardly need anyone to operate it. I've never seen it.
If you need a pound of sugar, you're not going to save any money buying single-wrapped cubes.

The internet got going in 1995. Cloud as the default choice didn't happen until maybe 2015. So for well over twenty years, companies have been operating hardware to run their applications. This isn't some archaic knowledge that's been lost to the ages.
We might not know exactly how the pyramids were built, but we do still know how to connect a Linux machine to the internet.

Moore's Law is hammering the prices and increasing the capabilities of stuff you buy from Dell and others. But it's scarcely moving the needle on the price you have to pay Amazon and others for their managed services.
Buy the baseline, rent the spike.
We bought the marketing pitch. That it was going to be cheaper, easier, and faster. Only the last promise ever really came through for us.
Typically if they have banks or governments on their client list, you ought to look elsewhere, unless you just like setting money on fire.
key_insight: Running your own hardware in the modern era of containerization and white-glove data center services is operationally comparable to cloud, dramatically cheaper at scale, and the cost gap widens every year as Moore's Law benefits hardware buyers but not cloud renters.
